topic: Someone receives an apology letter years after the incident
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope was thick, the paper brittle with age, and the handwriting was painstakingly neat. My hand shook so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk to keep it steady. It was from him. Years. Years had passed since the night everything fractured, since the silence had become a physical weight in my chest. Opening it felt like stepping into a freezing wind. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid, as my eyes scanned the familiar script. The words swam before my gaze, each line a sharp shard of memory twisting in my gut. I reread the opening sentence three times, my throat constricting. A strange, cold clamminess spread across my skin, making the fine hairs on my arms stand rigid. I couldn't move. My knuckles were white where I gripped the parchment, and I could feel the dull, heavy thudding of my own pulse echoing in the sudden, profound quiet of the room.

[story 2]

I hadn't expected the postman to bring anything. The thick, cream-colored paper felt alien in my hands, impossibly fragile. When I finally broke the seal, the ink seemed to bleed into the paper, a dark stain mirroring the turmoil inside me. My vision narrowed, focusing only on the elegant, looping script. Every word hit me like a physical blow, jarring my composure completely. I dropped the letter, and it landed on the floor with a soft, damning sound. I sank onto the edge of the bed, pulling my knees tight to my chest, trying to compress myself into nothingness. My chest rose and fell in shallow, uneven bursts. I squeezed my eyes shut, fighting the urge to bolt from the room, my entire body vibrating with a frantic, internal energy that made the air feel too thin to draw.

[story 3]

It arrived on a Tuesday, completely unannounced, leaving no note, just the heavy weight of the sealed envelope on my doorstep. I stared at it for a full minute, my mind refusing to process the reality of it. When I finally tore it open, the contents unfolded slowly, deliberately. A wave of nausea washed over me, so intense I had to press my palms against my eyes to keep the world from blurring. The sheer audacity of it—that someone, after all this time, had taken the time to craft this—sent a tremor straight through my core. I stood up abruptly, swaying slightly, my legs feeling disconnected from my body. I walked to the window, peering out at the streetlights, trying to anchor myself to something solid, but the silence inside me was deafening, punctuated only by the frantic drumming beneath my ribs.
